steps.title: Identify the mechanism the hypothesis must explain
steps.content: The bottleneck is that electrons must pass through a series of inefficient redox reactions before reaching the electrode, capping power at ~0.30 mW/cm². Adding highly conductive silver nanoparticles more than doubled power to 0.66 mW/cm². The hypothesis must explain how silver's conductivity overcomes the redox bottleneck.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'silver nanoparticles may increase the metabolic processes of the bacteria, thereby increasing the number of free electrons available to transfer to the electrode': The passage focuses on inefficient electron transport, not insufficient electron production — more electrons don't help if the bottleneck is the transfer pathway. ✗
  • Choice B — 'as the density of the biofilm increases, the series of redox reactions may accelerate independent of the presence of the silver nanoparticles': This attributes the power increase to biofilm density rather than silver conductivity, contradicting the experimental setup. ✗
  • Choice C — 'electrons may be conducted directly to the electrode before the silver nanoparticles catalyze the redox reactions': This reverses the logic — silver should bypass the redox reactions, not catalyze them; and "before" misframes the mechanism. ✗
  • Choice D — 'silver nanoparticles may allow electrons to bypass the series of redox reactions and transfer directly to the electrode': Silver's high conductivity creates a direct electron pathway around the inefficient redox reactions, directly explaining the power boost. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
